description: In October 2010, the clothing retailer Gap abruptly replaced its iconic blue-box logo with a minimalist Helvetica-based design, sparking intense public backlash and ridicule across social media. Following a massive wave of negative consumer sentiment, the company reverted to its original logo just six days after the initial rollout.

key_events.description: Gap launches new logo and requests crowdsourced design feedback on Facebook.
Viral mockery hits Twitter; 'Fake Gap Logo' accounts and parody generators trend globally.
Retail analysts label the rebrand a 'marketing disaster' and a threat to brand equity.
Gap corporate announces an immediate return to the classic blue-box logo after failing to appease customers.

calibration_notes: This scenario provides a perfect template for 'rapid-response' market failure. It is useful for calibrating how quickly negative sentiment can consolidate in a digital-first environment and how corporate entities handle the decision to pivot versus stay the course.
